data.meaning_mnemonic: This radical is the same as the kanji. It means <radical>attach</radical>.
This radical is the same as the kanji. It means <radical>valley</radical>.

This radical is made up of samurai and beans. Samurai used to put beans in <radical>drum</radical>s so they could tap and shake them to make noise. Can you imagine shaking and tapping this drum radical?

Which <kanji>day of the week</kanji> is it? I'll never tell you. It could be Sunday. It could be Monday. It could be any day of the week.

data.reading_mnemonic: Now that you know how to figure out what <kanji>day of the week</kanji> it is, you can start making <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). It can take up to 24 hours to make yogurt. Knowing which weekday it is at any given moment means you can perfect your yogurt-making schedule.
data.reading_hint: Picture yourself looking at the shadow cast by the two Wolverines and the turkey. Imagine waiting a whole 24 hours and knowing exactly when your yogurt is ready. All thanks to this makeshift sundial which can accurately tell you which day of the week it is.

data.reading_mnemonic: What did you <kanji>write</kanji> about? You were writing about this amazing circus <reading>show</reading> (しょ) you just watched on the sun. You didn't expect there to be any shows here, but turns out you were wrong!
data.reading_hint: Think of your favorite live shows and imagine them on the sun. Also imagine yourself writing back to your friends and family about said shows.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>scooter</radical> with a <radical>zombie</radical> on it, going really <kanji>far</kanji> away. Someone tied that zombie to the scooter and now it's far off in the distance, groaning and growling.

data.reading_hint: I mean, to be fair, the zombie wasn't really alive to begin with. But going far enough to end it all? That's some serious distance!

data.meaning_mnemonic: A psychic <radical>leader</radical> kneels to the <radical>ground</radical>, picks up a <radical>thread</radical>, and instantly makes a <kanji>connection</kanji> with the person that it came from. This leader is very powerful, so even a thread is enough for them to make a psychic connection with the person who was wearing it.

Your head spins, your heart races, and your hands tremble. Picture this happening to you as you pick up the thread from the ground.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The kanji is the same as the <radical>products</radical> radical, so it means <kanji>product</kanji> — you know, the kind of <kanji>article</kanji>s or <kanji>goods</kanji> you buy and sell.
data.meaning_hint: Which store has the best product selection, do you think? Picture all those articles and goods lined up on the shelves in their little boxes, kind of in the shape of this kanji.
data.reading_mnemonic: As you try to sell some <kanji>product</kanji>s, you finally get a buyer. It's a <reading>Hin</reading>du (ひん) person. She walks in and buys your products on the spot!
data.reading_hint: Then another Hindu person walks in your store and buys some. Then another... What are all these Hindu people doing buying your products, you wonder? When you ask them, they tell you "it's a cultural thing."

data.meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>roof</radical> needs <radical>measurement</radical>s done every year to make sure it will <kanji>protect</kanji> you properly.
data.meaning_hint: If you think about it, your roof protects you from a lot of things! Rain, snow, bird poop. But if your roof's measurements are off it won't be able to protect you properly.
data.reading_mnemonic: If the measurements are off, and your roof doesn't <kanji>protect</kanji> you, then you get to <reading>sue</reading> (す)! If you're still alive, that is.
data.reading_hint: Odds are, the roof collapsed or a tree fell on you and you died from lack of protection. So now your family and friends are going to sue on your behalf.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Put a <radical>lid</radical> on something <radical>spicy</radical> and you'll cook up pure <kanji>happiness</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Spicy foods literally do trigger happy chemicals in your brain. It wouldn't be too far off to say that putting a lid on a pot filled with something spicy could produce happiness.
data.reading_mnemonic: Out of spicy foods you made pure <kanji>happiness</kanji>. You give it to your sad friend <reading>こう</reading>いち, who waits at the table.
data.reading_hint: こういち looks sad. But when you serve him happiness, he starts to perk up a bit. A couple more bites of happiness, and there's a smile on his face. Although when you found こういち he was depressed, down on his luck, and hiding from the government, cooking him some spicy foods seems to have brought him some happiness.

data.meaning_mnemonic: In the <radical>prison</radical> where you once kept <radical>Wolverine</radical>, you find a beating <radical>heart</radical>. You don't understand how it's still going, but you know you need to <kanji>hurry</kanji> up and do something, or it might stop all of a <kanji>sudden</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Picture this beating heart in the prison. Was it Wolverine's? That would explain why it's, like, beating all supernaturally on its own. What should you do with it? You'd better hurry, its owner probably needs it.
data.reading_mnemonic: You start to overheat as you <kanji>hurry</kanji> around, so you grab a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう) to cool you down. The high water content re-hydrates you and lets you continue hurrying to help save this heart.
data.reading_hint: And if it really is Wolverine's heart, you're gonna have to be cool as a cucumber when you try to help him, even if you're in a hurry. He's emotionally cold, remember? Gotta play it cool with him, despite the emergency.

data.meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>pope</radical> stays fixated on the <radical>ground</radical>, staring at <radical>fins</radical>, he's going to lose touch with <kanji>reality</kanji>. He should be focusing on his popish duties, but instead he's looking at the fish fins he dropped, and now can't eat because they're dirty. Silly pope. You can't let this one thing get you down. You have other things to do.
data.meaning_hint: Picture trying to get the pope to stop staring at the fins lying on the dirty ground. Yup, he's completely lost touch with reality, you think.
data.reading_mnemonic: You're doing your best to bring the pope back to <kanji>reality</kanji> when he snaps and kicks you in the <reading>shin</reading> (しん). Oww! What the heck, pope! But he's already back staring at the fins and your shin is throbbing where he kicked it.
data.reading_hint: Imagine the pope angrily kicking your shin super hard. You fall over and pull it close. Feel how badly it aches. Who knew you'd need shin guards to deal with a pope that's lost touch with reality just from dropping his dinner.

data.meaning_mnemonic: With the <radical>bamboo</radical> from that <radical>tree</radical>, your trained <radical>eye</radical> can tell it'll make an excellent <kanji>box</kanji>. Boxes made from bamboo trees are very sturdy and fashionable. You have an eye for fashion as well, you see.

data.reading_mnemonic: Now you can use that <kanji>box</kanji> to store all your <reading>ha</reading>rd<reading>co</reading>re (はこ) punk vinyl records. You're a real connoisseur, so you know that hardcore music should only ever be kept in bamboo boxes.
data.reading_hint: Picture yourself filling up your lovingly crafted bamboo box with your collection of hardcore punk and rock records. Ah, they fit just perfectly thanks to that discerning eye of yours.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You offer <radical>flowers</radical> to your <radical>leader</radical> and compliment them on their <radical>lip ring</radical>. You think that by doing this they might carry your <kanji>luggage</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Picture this big, strong leader. They actually love flowers and feel flattered by your compliment! But being the leader, they can't help wondering why they should have to carry your luggage…

data.reading_hint: On top of being a strong leader, they also have a very kind heart, so your begging convinces them and they agree to carry your luggage.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>face</radical> radical and the <kanji>face</kanji> kanji are the same! But this isn't just a face like the one you're looking at your computer/phone with. It's the <kanji>surface</kanji> kind of face too!

data.reading_hint: Look at the face of all men put together. Measure how much surface area they all take up.

data.meaning_mnemonic: If you play <radical>music</radical> with your <radical>fins</radical>, one <kanji>rule</kanji> is that you must be a fish. You can’t play music using fish fins that don’t belong to you. That’s the rule. And it’s also rude.

data.reading_mnemonic: There’s also a <kanji>rule</kanji> in place to limit the amount of fish fin musicians. No more than <reading>ten</reading> (てん) are allowed to gather and play their music together. That’s the rule. No more than ten.
data.reading_hint: Picture a group of ten (as per the rule) fish playing music with their fins. Now the song can be louder and more intricate. Imagine it playing in your head.

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>canopy</radical> <radical>attach</radical>ing your entire country is the <kanji>government</kanji>. They loom over you all and try to keep you all organized and together under their control.
data.meaning_hint: Picture a map of the world with actual canopies attached to each government, representing the ruling governments of each place.
data.reading_mnemonic: Try to get out from under this <kanji>government</kanji> canopy and you'll be labeled a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). Unless... you can fool them!
data.reading_hint: Think of all the people who've been labeled as fools by the government because they try to detach themselves from under their reach. It's a pretty long list...

data.meaning_mnemonic:
After a <radical>tsunami</radical>, use this <radical>machine</radical> to <kanji>cure</kanji> the people and the country from the damage. <kanji>Heal</kanji>ing machines are all over Japan just in case a tsunami does way more damage than they can handle. 

Oh, and whoever cures and heals the most people will be able to <kanji>rule</kanji> over them once everything starts going back to normal. So keep that in mind, kay?
data.meaning_hint: Imagine if you had your own machine to cure yourself if a tsunami came to where you live. It would be pretty convenient!
data.reading_mnemonic:
Alternative ways to <kanji>cure</kanji> yourself after a tsunami are either praying to <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ) or eating your weight in <reading>chee</reading>se (ち). I'm not sure if I have enough cheese for all the people that got hit though... Hmm, do you have cheese?

Before you make your decision, remember that whoever cures you will be able to <kanji>reign</kanji> over you and your people. So do you want to be led by Jesus or by some cheese? The choice is yours.
data.reading_hint: Imagine getting Jesus to cure half the people and feeding the other half lots of cheese and their wounds miraculously healing on their own! You're a hero!

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits a <radical>valley</radical>, filling it up with water. Lucky for you, you're a giant, so you just lay down in the valley and use it to <kanji>bathe</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Sure, there's a lot of water in the valley, but you're big too. It's the perfect place for you to bathe... and good thing too, you're getting smelly.
data.reading_mnemonic: You know what would make the <kanji>bath</kanji>ing experience even better? If you had some egg <reading>yolk</reading>s (よく) to eat. Just crack those yolks into the water you bathe in, and it'll cook to perfection. Yum!
data.reading_hint: Imagine cooking egg yolks in the same water you bathe in.

data.meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Bamboo</radical> <radical>heaven</radical> is a place for <kanji>laugh</kanji>ing. Bamboo is a very jolly plant, and they love to laugh. Even after death in bamboo heaven, the bamboo continue to laugh joyfully. Bamboo heaven is truly a place of laughter.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself as a bamboo plant that laughs in heaven. What are you laughing about? Chuckle to yourself as you remember this kanji.
data.reading_mnemonic: There’s actually a war a goin’ on up in that there bamboo heaven, y’know? Ye might not have heard of it with all that there <kanji>laugh</kanji>in’ a goin’ on, but there definitely be a <reading>war a</reading> (わら) goin’ on up in bamboo heaven, y’hear? The bamboo are scared so that’s why they be laughin’ so much. They nervous.
data.reading_hint: Now picture these bamboo again up in bamboo heaven, and remember this strange accent to remember that the bamboo plants up in bamboo heaven are laughing with nervous laughter at this bamboo war that’s a goin’ on up there.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
When your <radical>tongue</radical> becomes too <radical>spicy</radical> for you to bear, it’s time to <kanji>quit</kanji> the jalapeño eating contest. You’ve been eating them for three hours now and the spiciness from the peppers is really burning your tongue. Throw in the towel, it’s time to quit the contest.

This kanji has another meaning too:

Eating isn't the only thing that makes your <radical>tongue</radical> <radical>spicy</radical>, so do certain <kanji>word</kanji>s. Say your favorite words out loud right now. Feel them on your tongue. Words are the spicy food of language!
data.meaning_hint: You may have to quit eating but you never have to quit saying words. Especially if you're, well, trying to learn a whole new language full of them.
data.reading_mnemonic: Oh, you're probably wondering who won that spicy food contest after you <kanji>quit</kanji> and became obsessed with <kanji>word</kanji>s instead. It was <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ). Who did you expect? He was great with words AND never quitting when things got spicy.
data.reading_hint: Picture Jesus scarfing down jalapeño peppers. He never quits. And then he says some words about how the jalapeños gave up their lives for this contest, so we better appreciate them. Thanks Jesus!

data.meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>gate</radical> of <radical>horns</radical> will take you to <radical>heaven</radical>. These horns are <kanji>related</kanji> and <kanji>connected</kanji> to heaven in some way.
data.meaning_hint: Perhaps they are related to heaven because the devil has horns? Perhaps they are connected to heaven because these are the horns of heaven's enemies? The real reason they're connected will be revealed in the reading mnemonic.
data.reading_mnemonic: They are <kanji>related</kanji> and <kanji>connected</kanji> to heaven because these are the horns that Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) made from his enemies. God said he could come into heaven if he made a gate of horns, so that's what he did.
data.reading_hint: Imagine Genghis Khan taking horns off his enemies, and making a gate to heaven. The horn gate is connected to both heaven and Genghis Khan in that way.

data.meaning_mnemonic: If you listened in to the <radical>private</radical> lives of <radical>twenty</radical> people, all of them would have different <kanji>dialect</kanji>s and ways of <kanji>speech</kanji>. Whether they actually use these dialects with you is another story. But when they're alone, or with other people who are from the same place, their speech will be different.
data.meaning_hint: Can you even imagine twenty different dialects in your head? Well I can tell you that there are definitely at least twenty of them in Japan alone.
data.reading_mnemonic: The best way to learn someone's <kanji>dialect</kanji> is to buy them a <reading>ben</reading>to (べん) from where they're from. Not only do these bento have writing in their dialect on them, but their speech will immediately shift to match. Then you'll be able to hear how they sound in their head and in private!
data.reading_hint: It also helps that bento are delicious, and anyone with any dialect will love them.

data.meaning_mnemonic: It's <radical>correct</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> to focus on <kanji>politics</kanji> and the <kanji>government</kanji>. Elections are happening, in the new year politicians are being sworn in. Winter is government time!
data.meaning_hint: Imagine all this politics and government talk as you shiver outside in the winter cold. You’re freezing and you’re bored, but you know this is the right (correct) thing to do.
data.reading_mnemonic: Everyone involved in <kanji>politics</kanji> and <kanji>government</kanji> wields a special <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). If two political parties are arguing, they'll whip out their sabers and start slashing away!
data.reading_hint: Picture two different politicians, both wielding sabers, fighting to make the government the way they want it to be! Who are you rooting for?

data.meaning_mnemonic: A group of <radical>private</radical> <radical>sword</radical> guys are in your <radical>rice paddy</radical>. You must <kanji>detain</kanji> them at once! Private sword guys are people who work for private companies and use their swords on people. They're basically paid assassins. And you're going to detain them!
data.meaning_hint: Imagine running into the rice paddy and detaining the private sword holders one by one. They won't get away this time!
data.reading_mnemonic:
You're running out of steam and you're afraid you might not be able to <kanji>detain</kanji> all of these guys when <reading>Ryu</reading> (りゅう) from Street Fighter appears. He hates swords, and will only use fists because it's the honorable way to fight, so he starts helping you detain them!

He's also found a <reading>loo</reading>phole (る) in the system and you don't have to be super gentle with these guys either. So detain them as forcefully as you want. It is your rice paddy they're in, after all.
data.reading_hint: If Ryu hadn't shown up with this loophole you never would have detained all these private sword guys by yourself!

data.meaning_mnemonic: What I <radical>say</radical> is <radical>correct</radical> 100%! You may use it as <kanji>evidence</kanji> in this murder case.
data.meaning_hint: You convince the officials questioning you that your evidence can be trusted and it's <kanji>proof</kanji> that you definitely didn't have anything to do with any law breaking or murdering.
data.reading_mnemonic: The officials force you to present this <kanji>evidence</kanji> in front of the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう), himself. Your knees quake, you feel like you're gonna be sick, but you try to stand up tall and tell him everything. After all, you know your <kanji>proof</kanji> is correct, so you shouldn't be so scared.
data.reading_hint: You better hope the Shougun believes your evidence is accurate, because he's known to kill people willy-nilly when he thinks they're lying.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Going into a <radical>building</radical> made by a <radical>squid</radical> is <kanji>risky</kanji>. First of all, squids only have tentacles, no bones, and they make all of their buildings out of risky materials like kelp and sharp coral.
data.meaning_hint: One squid got stuck in his own building, but no one could save him because going in would be too risky. Poor little squid.
data.reading_mnemonic: "It's too <kanji>risky</kanji>, leave me!" the squid cries. "I'll save you, underwater citizen! Nothing is too risky for <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai!" The building is collapsing on the squid, the risk is so great, but Ken the samurai dives in and saves him!
data.reading_hint:
The squid cries, "I can build one that isn't so risky next time!" Ken shakes his head, "I'm not letting you do anymore risky things, you're coming with me from now on." Ken scoops up the squid into a bucket, hoists him over his shoulder, and walks off into the sunset.

Who knows what adventures await our new duo. All we know is that they'll definitely be getting into some risky business.

data.meaning_mnemonic: There is a <radical>prison</radical> on a <radical>cliff</radical> surrounded by <radical>snake</radical>s. Now that's a <kanji>dangerous</kanji> place. If you try to escape the prison you either fall to your death off the cliff or get bitten/squeezed to death by the snakes. Can you think of anything more dangerous than this? I can't.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine a dangerous prison surrounded by cliffs and snakes. You wouldn't want to be here either, would you?
data.reading_mnemonic: Lucky for you, even though you're stuck in this <kanji>dangerous</kanji> place, there is hope. There is a <reading>key</reading> (き). All dangerous prisons have a key. You just have to find and steal it so you can avoid the cliffs and snakes!
data.reading_hint: Imagine staring at the key dangling right outside of the dangerous prison you're trapped in. Would you take the chance and go for it?

data.meaning_mnemonic: If a <radical>narwhal</radical> <radical>stick</radical>s a <radical>child</radical> with its horn then you have to admit that it <kanji>exist</kanji>s. We know some of you have been doubting narwhals and whether they're real. Well now your kid is a kabob, so do you believe they exist now? I <kanji>suppose</kanji> you do.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine needing this kind of horrible proof to believe something exists. Maybe you should have more faith in what we teach.
data.reading_mnemonic: Oh, and the child we had to narwhal stab to prove they <kanji>exist</kanji> - that's your <reading>son</reading> (そん). Maybe you should have listened and kept your son out of the Narwhal Danger <reading>Zone</reading> (ぞん) after we warned you about it.
data.reading_hint: I suppose you'll never forget that narwhals exist now that your son has one permanently stuck in him.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Sticking a <radical>cross</radical> into a <radical>rice paddy</radical> to get its <radical>measurement</radical>s is your <kanji>specialty</kanji>. Most people get really confused when they have to measure their rice paddies, but that's where you come in. You plant a cross right in the middle, look down it from one end to the other, and voila! You know the measurements immediately. That's why it's your specialty!
data.meaning_hint: Visualize yourself doing this. The numbers just come to you. It's just what you're good at. Your specialty is something no one else in the world is as good at as you!
data.reading_mnemonic: Hearing of your amazing <kanji>specialty</kanji> and cross measurement abilities, a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) from a far off land recruits you for a job. The centaur’s father has just passed away and the centaur wants you to measure the rice paddies that have been left to him.
data.reading_hint: Picture yourself at a centaur funeral. Tons of centaurs standing around and stamping their hooves as you make your measurement and stick a cross into the ground.

data.meaning_mnemonic: When the <radical>sun</radical> gets in your <radical>eye</radical>, I <kanji>dare</kanji> you to stare at it. I dare you to look right at the sun. I dare you.
data.meaning_hint: Looking at the sun straight on hurts. It’s bad for your eyes and burns your brain. Imagine how painful it is to stare at the sun.
data.reading_mnemonic: After you’ve blinded yourself from the <kanji>dare</kanji> of looking at the sun and losing sight in each eye, the guy dares you to go <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう). You’re blind now, so bowling will be quite the challenge.
data.reading_hint: Picture yourself bowling without the power of sight. It would be awfully difficult to do well. You’d just have to concentrate really hard on the sound of the pins and determine where you need to throw the ball.

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>forehead</radical> <radical>table</radical> is completely <kanji>superfluous</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Seriously, you don't need a forehead table. It's totally unnecessary to have a table made of foreheads and is a superfluous purchase. First of all, getting all those foreheads is a waste of people. Second, forehead tables aren't very strong. Superfluous, superfluous, superfluous.
data.reading_mnemonic: The only person you know with a <kanji>superfluous</kanji> forehead table is <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the ten foot tall, simple farmhand.
data.reading_hint: Now every time you visit Joe, you’ll have to look at his giant, superfluous forehead table. Imagine how gross it looks, with dozens of foreheads protruding from its surface. Only Joe would buy such a thing.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The place that <radical>buildings</radical> feel very <radical>anti</radical>- towards is <kanji>Osaka</kanji>. Osaka is the last place they want to be built. Why? data.meaning_mnemonic:
This is like a very formal and humble way of saying <vocabulary>to know</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to believe</vocabulary>. Sure, the kanji means <kanji>suppose</kanji>, but think of it this way... When you say "I suppose" it's like a humble way of saying "I know" or "I believe." You're not being as direct about it this way, and in Japanese, that makes you more humble and polite. So, that's why 存じる means <vocabulary>to know</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to believe</vocabulary>.

Note this word is typically used in the polite ます form. When you’re humble, you may want to be as polite as possible, I suppose!

data.reading_mnemonic:
The おおさか reading for this word is made up of the kun'yomi for each kanji. You've learned the おお reading (like in 大きい), but you haven't seen the さか reading for 阪 yet. 

That said, doesn't 阪 look a lot like 坂, which also means "slope"? Turns out they share the さか reading as well, so if you remember the word 坂 you can apply that reading here. (In fact, 大阪 was sometimes written as 大坂 in the old days.) The easiest mnemonic is probably just to think of the city of  <vocabulary>Osaka</vocabulary> though… Just don't forget about the double おお.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>closed</kanji> so the verb vocab version is <vocabulary>to close something</vocabulary>.

閉める primarily means <vocabulary>to shut something</vocabulary> in a physical sense (often with a sliding or twisting motion), like when you close doors, windows, curtains, faucets, and so on. Additionally, it can also be used for closing shops, either for the day or permanently.
data.reading_mnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

When you want <vocabulary>to close something</vocabulary>, use a <reading>shee</reading>p (し). Sheep are famously good closers. Doors, windows, curtains, you name it. Sure, they'll bleat on and on about how they're not your personal closer, they have their own doors to close, and bah bah blah, but they secretly love having the chance <vocabulary>to shut something</vocabulary> for you.

The hiragana ends with an う sound, so you know this is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>return</kanji>, and this verb is transitive, meaning it takes a direct object. You send someone back. You send them home. And tell them to never come near you again. So this means <vocabulary>to send someone back</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to send someone home</vocabulary>.

In fact, most verbs ending in す are transitive. To help you remember that, think of <reading>su</reading>ing (す) someone to make sure they go home!
data.reading_mnemonic:
The reading for this word is the same as the one you learned with the kanji. Yay! But since this word has a pair verb, let's think of a way to remember the ending:

What's the most surefire way to send someone home? Threaten to <reading>sue</reading> (す) them if they don't!

context_sentences.en: We're making sure to release the small mantis shrimps back into the sea.
context_sentences.ja: 小さいシャコは海に帰すようにしています。

context_sentences.ja: 今日は少し早く運転手を帰した。

context_sentences.ja: 作業が全部終わらないと帰してもらえないよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<reading>Who are who are</reading> (ふわふわ) <vocabulary>fluffy</vocabulary>? I’m thinking puppies, but ask yourself, "who are the fluffiest creatures I know?"

This is an オノマトペ meaning fluffy, like a puppy, a cloud, or a cotton ball. It can also describe the movement of a dandelion puffball or a jellyfish, <vocabulary>floating lightly</vocabulary> in the air or the water. And if you’re <vocabulary>feeling lightheaded</vocabulary>, that’s called ふわふわ too. Basically, anything with light or fluffy vibe!

context_sentences.en: Wow, this bread is so fluffy, isn't it!
context_sentences.ja: うわー、このパン、ふわふわだね！

context_sentences.en: Isn't it cute to see jellyfish floating in the sea?
context_sentences.ja: クラゲが海でふわふわ泳いでるのって、かわいくない？

context_sentences.en: I’m kind of light-headed.
context_sentences.ja: なんか頭がふわふわする。

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <kanji>bad</kanji> <kanji>change</kanji> is exactly what it sounds like: <vocabulary>deterioration</vocabulary>, aka <vocabulary>getting worse</vocabulary> .
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. The on'yomi readings of the kanji are あく and か, but here they become あっか, to make the word easier to pronounce. Try and say it out loud to see if you agree.

context_sentences.en: Stress is causing the disease to worsen quickly.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Remember how 問う means "to question something" and 合わせる means "to join together"? If you're joining your questions together, you're probably trying to <vocabulary>to inquire</vocabulary> about something. Inquiring is much more involved than plain old asking, after all.

問い合わせる is a phrase you can use when contacting a business or government office to request information or resolve an issue. It's a bit formal, but in an everyday business-speak kind of way, and it doesn't have the philosophical or weighty tone that 問う does.
data.reading_mnemonic:
You probably know how to read 問う and 合わせる already. 問う is still pretty new though, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember the 問 part:

You love <vocabulary>to inquire</vocabulary> with your <reading>toe</reading>s (と). Whenever you fill out one of those online forms to contact customer support with an inquiry, you do all your typing with your toes. They don't know you're making this inquiry with your toes — but you do, and it makes you chuckle.

context_sentences.en: I'll contact the manufacturer.
context_sentences.ja: メーカーに問い合わせるよ。

context_sentences.en: Why don't you try sending an inquiry by email?
context_sentences.ja: メールで問い合わせてみたら？

context_sentences.en: I wanted to inquire in advance, but I couldn't get through on the phone.
context_sentences.ja: 事前に問い合わせたかったんだけど、電話がつながらなかったんだよね。

data.meaning_mnemonic: Your <kanji>hand</kanji>s are <kanji>light</kanji> because it was a <vocabulary>simple and easy</vocabulary> task, so you didn't get your hands full. This word describes something that's hassle-free and doesn't take much effort, time, or sometimes cost to do.
data.reading_mnemonic: Both kanji use kun'yomi readings in this word, which you've already learned. Just make sure you rendaku the 軽.

context_sentences.en: Here's a quick and easy recipe where all you need to do is dip them in the sauce and microwave them.
context_sentences.ja: ソースにつけてレンジでチンするだけのお手軽レシピです。

context_sentences.en: We have created an application that makes it easy to collect "WaniKani Cards".

context_sentences.en: Let's move our bodies with easy exercises.
context_sentences.ja: 手軽にできるエクササイズで体を動かしましょう。

data.meaning_mnemonic: Remember how 生まれる means "to be born"? This word is similar, but it's used for when non-living things are "born". So as an intransitive verb, this means <vocabulary>to occur</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to arise</vocabulary>. When used transitively, it means <vocabulary>to cause something</vocabulary>. You'll probably encounter the intransitive use much more, though.
data.reading_mnemonic:
You'd think this would use kun'yomi, but it actually doesn't! Like other verbs ending in じる, this takes the on'yomi reading of the kanji. 生 has two of those, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use: 

If you wanted anything <vocabulary>to occur</vocabulary> in medieval Japan, you needed to talk to the <reading>Shou</reading>gun. You always needed the Shougun's help <vocabulary>to cause something</vocabulary>. Without the Shougun, nothing would ever happen!

context_sentences.en: If communication isn't done well, some issues might arise.
context_sentences.ja: コミュニケーションが上手くできていないと、トラブルが生じるかもしれません。

context_sentences.en: From there, many problems emerged.
context_sentences.ja: そこから多くの問題が生じました。

context_sentences.en: Due to system updates, it will be necessary to temporarily halt the system.
context_sentences.ja: システムのアップデートにより一時的にシステムを止める必要が生じます。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You know the two words that make up this word, 行 and 列, and they both have similar meanings: a line and a row. Put them together and you have a <vocabulary>line</vocabulary> or a <vocabulary>queue</vocabulary>.

This usually means a line of people waiting for their turn, for example to enter a restaurant. It can be also used for a line that marches, like a line in a parade. In a mathematics context, it can refer to a <vocabulary>matrix</vocabulary>, which makes sense because they're lines and rows of numbers and stuff.
data.reading_mnemonic: This word takes the readings of the two words that make it up, so you should be lining up to read it!

context_sentences.en: I found a ramen restaurant that has a line.
context_sentences.ja: 行列のできるラーメン屋を見つけた。

context_sentences.en: Oh, a line of ants!
context_sentences.ja: あ、アリの行列だ！

context_sentences.en: I lined up in a queue at midnight to buy concert tickets.
context_sentences.ja: コンサートのチケットを買うために、夜中から行列にならびました。

context_sentences.en: I learned about matrices today in math class.
context_sentences.ja: 今日は数学のクラスで行列について学びました。

auxiliary_meanings.meaning: To Put On Top Of Another

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You've got lots of <kanji>heavy</kanji> things, so the only logical thing is <vocabulary>to stack</vocabulary> them all up. 

Besides heavy physical things, you can also use this word when you're going <vocabulary>to accumulate</vocabulary> things like experience, practice, or years on your back.
data.reading_mnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

All the things you've <vocabulary>stack</vocabulary>ed up were in preparation for your new <reading>casa</reading> (かさ). With everything inside boxes and neatly piled up, you're finally ready to move into your new casa. Hooray!

context_sentences.en: It is dangerous to stack so many plates.
context_sentences.ja: そんなにお皿を重ねると危ないですよ。

context_sentences.en: The princess became more and more beautiful as she grew older.
context_sentences.ja: プリンセスは、年を重ねるごとに美しくなっていきました。

context_sentences.en: Isn't it a waste of time to have a bunch of discussions about things that don't have answers?
context_sentences.ja: 答えのないことにディスカッションを重ねるのってムダじゃないですか？

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <kanji>neck</kanji> <kanji>minister</kanji>? This meaning <vocabulary>prime minister</vocabulary> makes sense if you think about the neck being the most important part of the body, kind of like the head!
data.reading_mnemonic:
This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. It also uses a reading for 相 that you haven't learned yet, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

The first <vocabulary>prime minister</vocabulary>s of Japan were actually the <reading>sho</reading>guns (しょう)… kind of.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
What's <kanji>new</kanji> and <kanji>green</kanji>? That'd be the <vocabulary>new greenery</vocabulary> you see on trees and other plants every year!

新緑 refers to <vocabulary>new foliage</vocabulary> — fresh green leaves of late spring and early summer, just after they've fully emerged. In Japan, it's seen as a symbol of that time of year, so it often carries a slightly poetic nuance.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. You've seen them before (remember the りょく reading from 緑茶?), so you should be good to go here.

context_sentences.en: Please come again when the new greenery is out.
context_sentences.ja: 新緑の時期にまた来てくださいね。

context_sentences.en: April and May are definitely the months when the new foliage is most beautiful.
context_sentences.ja: 新緑が一番きれいなのはやっぱり四月と五月ですよ。

context_sentences.en: I feel like my eyesight improved just from gazing at the mountains covered in new green leaves.
context_sentences.ja: 新緑の山をずーっと見てたら目が良くなった気がするんだ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You see a <kanji>row</kanji> of <kanji>car</kanji>s lined up neatly. Are they stuck in traffic? Nope. They're train cars. Congratulations, you've found a <vocabulary>train</vocabulary>!

You learned 電車 means "train," but it technically just refers to electric ones. On the other hand, 列車 is the broader, all-trains-welcome term. Diesel? Steam? Fancy long-distance express? All covered. But 列車 sounds more formal, so while 電車 is an everyday word, you may come across 列車 more often in writing or in the official descriptions used by railway companies.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. Just be sure to note the れつ reading, which gets changed to れっ.

context_sentences.ja: 列車はゆっくりと動き出しました。

context_sentences.en: Have you ever taken a night train?

context_sentences.en: When the train entered the tunnel, only the sound grew louder.
